Guideline: Prefer LazyVStack over VStack for long lists
Description: VStack renders all children immediately into memory; LazyVStack only renders visible items and deallocs off-screen rows
Do: Use LazyVStack inside ScrollView for lists with 20+ items; use List for standard list UX (selection swipe); set pinnedViews for sticky headers
Don't: Use VStack.children with 100+ items; use ForEach inside VStack for dynamic content lists
Code Good: ScrollView { LazyVStack(alignment: .leading, spacing: 0) { ForEach(items) { item in ItemRow(item: item) } } }
Code Bad: ScrollView { VStack { ForEach(items) { item in ItemRow(item: item) } } }  // All items built at once

Description: SwiftUI calls body frequently for any state change anywhere in the subtree; heavy body computation causes dropped frames
Do: Extract sub-views into separate structs (each has its own rendering cycle); use @State with minimal scope; avoid side effects in body
Don't: Fetch data compute arrays sort/filter inside body computed property; make network calls in onAppear without async task
Code Good: struct ItemRow: View { let item: Item; var body: some View { HStack { Text(item.title); Spacer(); Text(item.price) } } }  // Extracted = own rendering cycle
Code Bad: struct ContentView: View { var body: some View { let sorted = items.sorted(); return List(sorted) { ... } }  // sorted() runs every body call

Description: NavigationView is deprecated in iOS 16; NavigationStack provides type-safe programmatic navigation with NavigationPath
Do: Use NavigationStack with path binding for programmatic navigation; .navigationDestination(for:) for type-safe routing; NavigationSplitView for iPad
Don't: Use NavigationView (deprecated); present screens by binding showDetail without navigation stack path
Code Good: @State private var path = NavigationPath(); NavigationStack(path: $path) { ContentView().navigationDestination(for: Item.self) { item in DetailView(item: item) } }

Guideline: Handle deep links via onOpenURL and .navigationDestination
Description: SwiftUI handles deep links via the scene phase and URL opening; properly map URL to navigation state
Do: Use .onOpenURL modifier on root view; update NavigationPath based on URL; decode path parameters; test with Simulator > Device > Open URL
Don't: Rely on AppDelegate for URL handling when using SwiftUI scene lifecycle; ignore back stack considerations for deep links
Code Good: ContentView().onOpenURL { url in if let itemId = parseItemId(from: url) { path.append(Item(id: itemId)) } }
Code Bad: func application(_ app: UIApplication, open url: URL, ...) { ... }  // UIKit pattern in SwiftUI app

Guideline: Use @StateObject for mutable reference types — not @ObservedObject
Description: @ObservedObject recreates the object on parent rebuild losing state; @StateObject creates once and retains through re-renders
Do: Use @StateObject for ViewModels owned by the view; @ObservedObject for ViewModels passed in; @EnvironmentObject for deeply shared state
Don't: Use @ObservedObject for a ViewModel created in the view (recreated on every parent rebuild losing work)
Code Good: @StateObject private var viewModel = HomeViewModel()  // Created once retained for view lifetime
Code Bad: @ObservedObject var viewModel = HomeViewModel()  // Recreated every parent rebuild — data lost

Description: UserDefaults persistence usually requires boilerplate wrapper; @AppStorage ties directly to UserDefaults key with automatic observation
Do: @AppStorage for user preferences small settings; never for large data (use Core Data or files)
Don't: Use @AppStorage for URLs Data binary Data arrays; use for megabytes of data (use file storage instead)
Code Good: @AppStorage('isDarkMode') private var isDarkMode: Bool = false; Toggle('Dark Mode', isOn: $isDarkMode)

Guideline: Use safeAreaInset for content adjacent to safe area boundaries
Description: safeAreaInset adds content in the safe area without obscuring main content — perfect for persistent overlay bars
Do: Use .safeAreaInset(edge:.bottom) for floating bottom bars search bars or mini-players; doesn't require manual padding calc
Don't: Add hardcoded bottom padding to accommodate overlapping views; use ZStack with manual safe area calculation
Code Bad: ZStack { MainContent().padding(.bottom, 80); VStack { Spacer(); MiniPlayerBar() } }  // Manual and fragile

Guideline: Use GeometryReader sparingly — extract to separate view
Description: GeometryReader forces a size measurement pass and can cause layout ambiguity if overused; it passes available space
Do: Use only when truly needed (proportional sizing overlay placement); extract GeometryReader into leaf view not parent
Don't: Wrap large view trees in GeometryReader for minor size info; nest multiple GeometryReaders; use in List rows
Code Good: struct AdaptiveRow: View { var body: some View { GeometryReader { geo in HStack { content.frame(width: geo.size.width * 0.7) } } } }
Code Bad: GeometryReader { geo in LazyVStack { ForEach(items) { item in Row(item:item, width:geo.size.width) } } }  // Redundant expensive

Description: Unlabeled interactive elements are announced as 'button' or 'image' with no context by VoiceOver
Do: accessibilityLabel: what the element IS (noun phrase); accessibilityHint: what happens on activation (verb phrase); hide decorative with .accessibilityHidden(true)
Don't: Use label for instruction (put that in hint); use redundant labels that just repeat the button text
Code Bad: Image('heart.fill').accessibilityLabel('Double tap to like')  // Instructions belong in hint not label

Description: VoiceOver Rotor allows navigation by headings; unmarked visual headings are not navigable via rotor
Do: Add .accessibilityAddTraits(.isHeader) to every section/screen heading; H1 equivalent for screen title; H2 for section headers
Don't: Apply header trait only to visually large text ignoring semantic hierarchy; mark every bold text as header
Code Bad: Text('Today').font(.title2.bold())  // VoiceOver cannot navigate to this by heading

Guideline: Use matchedGeometryEffect for shared element transitions
Description: matchedGeometryEffect syncs position/size/shape of two views across a namespace enabling hero-style transitions within a view hierarchy
Do: Create @Namespace; apply .matchedGeometryEffect(id:,in:) to matching source and target; use with withAnimation
Don't: Use matchedGeometryEffect with different IDs on source/target (no effect); use across NavigationStack (use Hero in UIKit for that)
Code Good: @Namespace private var ns; ForEach(items) { item in if selected == item { DetailCard().matchedGeometryEffect(id:item.id, in:ns) } else { GridCard().matchedGeometryEffect(id:item.id, in:ns) } }
Code Bad: Animation { show.toggle() }  // No shared element — just a crossfade not a hero

Description: Some users experience motion sickness or disorientation from animations; iOS provides Reduce Motion setting that apps must honor
Do: Check UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled; use .animation(.default) not spring for reduced; offer fade instead of slide; test with Reduce Motion on
Don't: Ignore Reduce Motion and play full animations always; only reduce animations for reduce motion but keep micro-interactions
Code Bad: withAnimation(.spring(response:0.4)) { expanded.toggle() }  // Always springs even if user disabled motion

Guideline: Use AsyncImage with placeholder for network images
Description: SwiftUI's AsyncImage handles async loading but defaults to empty content during load; provide placeholder for good UX
Do: Provide placeholder and failure states in AsyncImage; cache loaded images via URLCache; use .resizable().scaledToFill() inside phase
Don't: Leave AsyncImage with no placeholder (blank space during load); load high-resolution originals for thumbnails (use CDN variants)
Code Good: AsyncImage(url: URL(string: imageUrl)) { phase in switch phase { case .empty: ShimmerView(); case .success(let img): img.resizable().scaledToFill(); case .failure: Color.gray; @unknown default: EmptyView() } }
Code Bad: AsyncImage(url: URL(string: url)) { img in img.resizable() } placeholder: { EmptyView() }  // No loading state

Guideline: Use Dynamic Type font styles not fixed sizes
Description: Fixed font sizes don't respond to user's text size preference in system settings; Dynamic Type styles automatically scale
Do: Use Font.headline Font.body Font.caption etc.; custom fonts: Font.custom('Brand',size:17,relativeTo:.body)
Don't: Hardcode font size in points; scale factor 1.0 clamping; ignore extraSmall and Accessibility sizes
Code Good: Text(title).font(.headline)  // Scales with user's font size preference
Code Bad: Text(title).font(.system(size: 17))  // Fixed -- stays at 17 regardless of user font size

Guideline: Define adaptive colors for light and dark mode
Description: Hardcoded colors look wrong in dark mode; SwiftUI Color assets with appearances handle both automatically
Do: Define colors in Asset Catalog with Any/Dark appearances; use Color('MyBrand') or .primary .secondary .background
Don't: Use Color(red:0.1,green:0.1,blue:0.1) in dark-mode app (looks invisible on dark background)
Code Good: Color('BrandAccent')  // Asset catalog with light + dark appearance defined
Code Bad: Color(red: 0.12, green: 0.12, blue: 0.12)  // Static color -- invisible in dark mode if background is also dark

Guideline: Write ViewInspector or XCTest UI tests for critical flows
Description: SwiftUI views are harder to unit-test than UIKit; XCTest UI tests cover full integration; ViewInspector for unit-level
Do: Use XCUITest for critical user journeys (login search checkout); ViewInspector for unit-level view logic verification
Don't: Skip UI testing assuming logic tests are enough; write UI tests without proper waitsForExistence calls
Code Good: let app = XCUIApplication(); app.launch(); app.buttons['Sign In'].tap(); XCTAssertTrue(app.staticTexts['Welcome'].waitForExistence(timeout:5))
Code Bad: app.buttons['Sign In'].tap(); XCTAssertTrue(app.staticTexts['Welcome'].exists)  // Race condition without wait

Guideline: Use Result type and Swift concurrency (async/await) for errors
Description: Callback-based APIs with untyped errors produce uncaught exceptions; Result + async/await express errors in the type system
Do: Use async throws functions; catch specific Error types; display user-facing error messages; propagate via task result
Don't: Ignore returned Errors; use try! in production code; show technical error messages to users
Code Good: func loadPosts() async throws -> [Post] { ... } Task { do { posts = try await loadPosts() } catch let error as NetworkError { showError(error.localizedDescription) } }
Code Bad: let posts = try! await loadPosts()  // Force try crashes production
